sections.title: In the exam (1)
sections.content: In FCE this is **Part 4**. You get two sentences; the second has a gap and a **key word** in capitals. You must complete the second sentence so it means the same as the first, using the key word. You usually write **between two and five words**.

sections.title: In the exam (2)
sections.content: **Tip:** The key word often tells you which grammar to use (e.g. SINCE → present perfect, SO → result).

sections.title: What is it? (1)
sections.content: You get **two sentences**. The first sentence is complete. The second has a **gap** and a **key word** in capitals. You must finish the second sentence so it **means the same** as the first. You must use the key word.

sections.title: What is it? (2)
sections.content: You usually write **two to five words** in the gap (including the key word). This task tests grammar and vocabulary.

sections.title: What to do (1)
sections.content:
• Read the **first sentence**. Understand what it means.
• Look at the **key word**. It often shows you which grammar to use (e.g.

sections.title: What to do (2)
sections.content:
SINCE → present perfect; SO → result).
• Finish the **second sentence** so it has the **same meaning** as the first.
• Use **two to five words**. Do not change the key word.

sections.title: Examples (1)
sections.content:
• **SINCE**: I last saw him in May → I haven't **seen** him since May.
• **MIGHT**: It's possible she forgot → She **might have** forgotten.
• **WISH**: I regret not studying → I **wish I had studied**.
• **Passive**: They built the house in 1990 → T

questions.question: Can you change the key word (e.g. write 'since' instead of 'SINCE')?

options.a: Yes, you can change the form
options.b: No, you must use the key word exactly as given

questions.explanation: You must use the key word and you cannot change its form.

questions.question: How many words do you usually write in the gap (FCE)?
questions.explanation: FCE: usually two to five words in the gap.

questions.question: The second sentence must mean _____ the first.
questions.explanation: The second sentence must have the same meaning as the first.

questions.question: I last saw him in May. SINCE → I haven't _____ him since May. What goes in the gap?
questions.explanation: Present perfect: haven't seen (from a point in the past until now).

questions.question: What is a good first step?

options.b: Read the first sentence and understand its meaning

questions.explanation: Understanding the first sentence helps you write the same meaning in the second.

questions.question:
I last saw him in May. SINCE
I haven't _____ him since May.
questions.explanation: Present perfect: haven't seen (from a point in time until now).

questions.question:
It's possible that she forgot. MIGHT
She _____ forgotten.
questions.explanation: Possibility in the past: might have + past participle.

questions.question:
They are building a new school. BEING
A new school is _____ built.
questions.explanation: Passive present continuous: is being built.

questions.question:
I don't have enough money to buy a car. AFFORD
I can't _____ a car.

We couldn't go out because of the rain. PREVENTED
The rain _____ going out.

She started working here three years ago. FOR
She has been working here _____ three years.
questions.explanation: For + a period of time (how long).

questions.question:
Nobody in the class is taller than Maria. TALLEST
Maria is _____ in the class.

I regret not studying harder. WISH
I _____ harder.
questions.explanation: I wish + past perfect (regret about the past).

questions.question:
They cancelled the match. CALLED
The match was _____ because of the weather.

It wasn't necessary for you to bring a gift. NEED
You _____ brought a gift.
questions.explanation: Needn't have + past participle = it wasn't necessary (but you did).
